items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram?
items.why: Students assume carbs (the 'energy food') provide most energy per gram. Carbs and protein = 4 kcal/g; FAT = 9 kcal/g (most energy-dense). Vitamins provide no energy at all.

items.q: Are denaturation and coagulation two words for the same change?
items.correct: No - denaturation is the unfolding of protein structure; coagulation is the later stage where unfolded proteins bond together and set.
items.misconception: Yes - they are identical and interchangeable terms.
items.why: Exam answers often lose marks here. Heat, acid or whisking first DENATURES proteins (unravels them). The exposed chains can then link to one another and set solid - COAGULATION. Frying an egg shows both stages in sequence.

items.q: Will starch thicken a cold liquid on its own?
items.correct: No - gelatinisation needs both liquid AND heat (starting around 60°C) for the granules to swell and burst.
items.misconception: Yes - stirring flour into cold water thickens it just like a heated sauce.
items.why: Students see flour 'disappear' into cold water and assume it has thickened. Cold starch granules merely suspend (and settle out). Only with heat do granules absorb liquid, swell and rupture, releasing starch molecules that thicken the sauce.

items.q: Egg white turning opaque on heating is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Coagulation. “Gelatinisation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximate temperature in °C when gelatinisation begins?
items.why: The real figure is 60. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
